s5.title: Compliance with Personal Data Protection Principles
s5.auditorFocus: JPDP inspectors will verify that the organisation has a functioning compliance programme covering all seven principles, with demonstrable accountability at senior management level. The absence of a DPO, a compliance policy, or a ROPA are among the most reliable indicators of systemic non-compliance.

evidenceItems.name: Record of Processing Activities (ROPA)
evidenceItems.description: A comprehensive register of all personal data processing activities conducted by the organisation, mapping each activity to its applicable principles, lawful basis, data categories, purposes, and responsible owner
whatGoodLooksLike: ROPA is maintained in a structured format with a named owner for each entry
Every processing activity has an identified lawful basis under s6(2) with supporting documentation
ROPA shows the last-reviewed date and is updated whenever new processing activities commence
Processing activities are mapped to all seven principles with notes on how compliance is achieved
ROPA version history is maintained showing evolution over time
commonGaps: ROPA has not been updated since initial creation and no longer reflects actual processing activities
ROPA exists but contains no lawful basis analysis — just a list of data types
Processing activities added after the initial ROPA creation are not recorded
No named owner for individual processing activities, making accountability unclear
suggestedSources: ROPA document or spreadsheet (current version and prior versions)
Change log or version history for the ROPA
Data governance meeting minutes approving ROPA updates
evidenceItems.retentionPeriod: Current version plus previous 3 years of versions

evidenceItems.name: PDPA Compliance Policy (Board-Approved)
evidenceItems.description: A formal policy document approved by the Board or equivalent governing body articulating the organisation's commitment to PDPA compliance and the accountability framework
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy is approved and signed by the Board Chair or CEO with a clear approval date
Policy explicitly addresses each of the seven Personal Data Protection Principles
Named accountability for each principle and for overall PDPA compliance is specified
Policy includes a review schedule (at least annual) and shows evidence of previous reviews
Policy is communicated to all staff and accessible on the intranet or equivalent
commonGaps: No Board-approved policy exists — only operational-level procedures
Policy exists but has not been reviewed since the 2024 Amendment came into force
Policy is not communicated to staff and cannot be shown to have been distributed
Policy exists but accountability assignments are vacant or outdated
suggestedSources: Board resolution or minutes approving the PDPA compliance policy
Signed PDPA compliance policy document (current version)
Distribution records or email showing policy communication to staff
evidenceItems.format: PDF / Word document with Board approval evidence
evidenceItems.retentionPeriod: Current version plus all prior versions indefinitely

evidenceItems.name: Annual Compliance Audit Report
evidenceItems.description: A report documenting the results of an annual audit of PDPA compliance across all seven principles, including findings, gap analysis, and remediation actions
whatGoodLooksLike: Audit covers all seven principles with specific findings and evidence for each
Report identifies gaps with risk ratings and assigns remediation actions to named owners with target dates
Findings from prior year's audit include status updates showing remediation progress
Audit methodology is documented including systems reviewed, staff interviewed, and documents examined
Report is presented to senior management or Board with formal acknowledgement
commonGaps: No annual compliance audit has ever been conducted
Audit covers only a subset of principles without addressing all seven
Findings from prior audits remain open with no evidence of remediation
Report exists but was never presented to management, suggesting it was not acted upon
suggestedSources: Annual compliance audit report (most recent and prior year)
Management sign-off on audit report
Remediation tracker showing status of prior findings

auditTips: Ask for the ROPA and test a sample of 5 processing activities by requesting supporting documentation for the claimed lawful basis
Check the DPO appointment letter — is the DPO named in the ROPA? Can they explain the seven principles?
Ask when the PDPA compliance policy was last reviewed — was this after the 2024 Amendment came into force?
Request the last Board or senior management meeting minutes where PDPA compliance was reported

s6.title: General Principle
s6.auditorFocus: JPDP inspectors will verify that the organisation has a documented lawful basis for each personal data processing activity, with particular attention to consent records and their validity. The 2024 Amendment's strengthened consent definition means consent collected before April 2025 should be reviewed against the new standard.

evidenceItems.name: Consent records database or log
evidenceItems.description: Centralised system or records showing when, how, and what consent was obtained from each data subject, including the specific purposes consented to
whatGoodLooksLike: Timestamped consent records with a clear link to the specific processing purposes consented to
Records show the exact wording or version of the consent form presented to each data subject
Separate consent records for separate processing purposes (granular consent)
Withdrawal records are integrated, showing when consent was withdrawn and what processing stopped
commonGaps: Consent collected via pre-ticked boxes — records exist but consent is invalid under the 2024 Amendment
No record of when or how consent was obtained; only a flag showing 'consented: yes'
Single bundled consent record for multiple unrelated purposes with no granularity
Consent records show data subject provided consent but no record of what wording they were shown
suggestedSources: CRM system consent module export
Website or app consent management platform (CMP) audit log
Paper consent forms with collection date
Marketing database opt-in/opt-out history export

evidenceItems.name: Lawful basis register
evidenceItems.description: A document mapping each processing activity to its s6(2) lawful basis, with supporting evidence for each basis claimed
whatGoodLooksLike: Each processing activity is mapped to a specific subsection of s6(2)
For contract basis: a sample contract evidencing the data subject's party status is referenced
For legitimate interests basis: a documented Legitimate Interest Assessment (LIA) is attached
Register is reviewed at least annually and upon any new processing activity
commonGaps: Processing activities list 'consent' as the lawful basis but no consent records can be produced
Multiple processing activities claim 'legitimate interests' without any LIA
Lawful basis claimed does not match the actual circumstances
Register has never been reviewed since initial creation
suggestedSources: Lawful basis register (standalone or as column in ROPA)
Legitimate Interest Assessments for LI-based processing
Sample consent forms evidencing the standard used

evidenceItems.name: Consent withdrawal mechanism and records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that data subjects can withdraw consent and that withdrawals are processed promptly with cessation of the relevant processing
whatGoodLooksLike: Unsubscribe/opt-out links in marketing communications are functioning
Withdrawal request log shows requests received and the date processing ceased for each
Time between withdrawal and cessation does not exceed a reasonable period
Withdrawal of consent triggers notification to third parties who received the data under the same consent
commonGaps: Unsubscribe links in emails are broken or lead to a 404 error
Withdrawal requests are acknowledged but processing does not actually stop
Third parties are not notified of withdrawal of consent
No log of withdrawal requests and their outcomes
suggestedSources: Marketing platform suppression list and unsubscribe history
Withdrawal request log from CRM or DPO inbox
Test of unsubscribe mechanism (live test during inspection)
Emails to third parties notifying of consent withdrawals
evidenceItems.format: System screenshots / Database export / Email evidence

auditTips: Request a sample of 20 consent records across different processing purposes and verify each against the four elements of valid consent: freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous
Test the unsubscribe link in a recent marketing email during the inspection to verify it functions
Ask for the Legitimate Interest Assessment for the most significant LI-based processing activity
Check whether consent forms were updated after April 2025 to reflect the new definition of consent

s7.title: Notice and Choice Principle
s7.auditorFocus: JPDP inspectors will review all privacy notices against the s7(1) checklist. Common findings include missing bilingual notices, outdated notices that do not reflect current processing, and notices that omit mandatory elements such as the right of access or contact details.

evidenceItems.name: Privacy notice (current versions — BM and English)
evidenceItems.description: The current versions of the organisation's privacy notice in both Bahasa Malaysia and English, covering all s7(1) elements, for each data collection channel
whatGoodLooksLike: Both BM and English versions are substantively identical and dated with the last-updated date
Notice explicitly covers all s7(1) elements: description of data collected, purposes, third party recipients, access/correction rights, contact details, obligatory/voluntary status
Notice is accessible at every data collection point
Plain language is used — a layperson can understand the key information
Version history is maintained
commonGaps: Notice is available in English only, without a Bahasa Malaysia version
Notice omits one or more mandatory s7(1) elements
Notice has not been updated to reflect new processing purposes or third-party disclosures
BM and English versions differ in substance
suggestedSources: Website privacy policy (current and archived versions)
Mobile app privacy notice screenshots
Paper consent and data collection forms
Point-of-sale notice boards (photos)

evidenceItems.name: Notice distribution and communication records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that privacy notices were actively communicated to data subjects at the point of collection and that existing data subjects were informed of material changes
whatGoodLooksLike: Email notifications to existing data subjects when a material privacy notice update was made
Registration forms include a checkbox or notice requiring acknowledgement of the privacy notice before submission
Signed forms where the data subject's signature acknowledges receipt of the notice
Notification campaigns for significant changes are documented with send dates and recipient counts
commonGaps: Privacy notice is updated on the website but existing data subjects are never notified
Online registration forms do not include any reference to the privacy notice
No record exists of when any version of the notice was communicated to any data subject
Paper forms do not include the privacy notice
suggestedSources: Email campaign records showing privacy notice update communications
Registration form screenshots showing privacy notice acknowledgement
Signed paper consent forms with notice acknowledgement
evidenceItems.format: Email records / CRM screenshots / Signed forms
evidenceItems.retentionPeriod: Duration of data subject relationship + 7 years

evidenceItems.name: Opt-out / choice mechanism evidence
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that data subjects have been provided with a genuine and functioning choice to opt out of non-essential processing
whatGoodLooksLike: Marketing consent mechanism clearly separates essential consent from optional marketing consent
Opt-out mechanism is as prominent and accessible as the corresponding opt-in
Marketing platform shows suppressed/opted-out contacts with no marketing communications sent to them
commonGaps: A single checkbox covers consent to both service delivery and marketing with no separate opt-out
Opted-out contacts continue to receive some categories of marketing communications
Opt-out preferences are not synchronised across all marketing channels
suggestedSources: Screenshots of consent mechanism in registration forms or checkout flows
Marketing platform export showing opted-out contacts and their opt-out dates
Multi-channel suppression consistency test results
evidenceItems.retentionPeriod: Duration of data subject relationship + 7 years

auditTips: Use the s7(1) element checklist against the actual privacy notices — verify each of the 7 required elements is present in both BM and English
Check the date on the privacy notice — was it updated after the 2024 Amendment came into force?
Navigate to the organisation's website and test the registration journey — is the privacy notice visible and acknowledged before data submission?
Ask to see the opt-out mechanism and test whether it functions immediately

s8.title: Disclosure Principle
s8.auditorFocus: JPDP inspectors will focus on third-party data sharing, whether data processing agreements are in place, and whether disclosures align with what was notified to data subjects. Cross-border transfers are a particular area of focus following the 2024 Amendment.

evidenceItems.name: Third-party disclosure register
evidenceItems.description: A register documenting all third parties to whom personal data has been disclosed, the categories of data shared, the purpose, the legal basis, and the contractual mechanism
whatGoodLooksLike: Register identifies each third party by name, categories of personal data shared, purpose, and specific s8(1) basis
DPA reference number is recorded for each third-party recipient who is a data processor
Register is updated whenever a new third-party disclosure arrangement is established
Cross-border transfers are flagged with the applicable s129 mechanism
commonGaps: No third-party disclosure register exists
Register exists but major vendors receiving personal data are not listed
Third-party recipients listed in the privacy notice do not match the register
suggestedSources: Third-party disclosure register
Vendor management system listing all vendors with data access
IT systems inventory showing all integrations and API connections

evidenceItems.name: Data Processing Agreements (DPAs) with data processors
evidenceItems.description: Executed written agreements with all data processors containing the mandatory terms required by s43
whatGoodLooksLike: DPAs are executed with all data processors, predating the commencement of processing
DPAs contain mandatory clauses: instruction-only processing, confidentiality, security standards, sub-processor controls, breach notification, and post-contract deletion
DPAs are current (not expired), and renewal dates are tracked
commonGaps: No DPAs in place with major data processors
DPAs exist but are general-purpose NDAs without specific data protection clauses
DPAs have expired and have not been renewed
suggestedSources: Executed DPA documents for all processors
Processor register cross-referenced with DPA file
DPA renewal calendar and tracking records

auditTips: Request the full list of vendors with access to personal data and cross-reference against DPA files
Review a sample of 5 DPAs against the s43 requirements checklist
Compare third-party recipients in the privacy notice against the third-party disclosure register

s9.title: Security Principle
s9.auditorFocus: JPDP inspectors assess the Security Principle against the Personal Data Protection Standards 2015 (Security Standard). The 2024 Amendment's breach notification requirements make security governance even more critical.

evidenceItems.name: Data Security Policy
evidenceItems.description: A written, senior management-approved security policy covering the protection of personal data
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy is approved and signed by senior management with a clear approval and review date
Policy addresses both electronic and non-electronic personal data processing
Policy includes: encryption, access controls, incident response, staff training, and third-party processor security
Policy has been reviewed and updated after the 2024 Amendment came into force
Policy is communicated to all relevant staff
commonGaps: No written data security policy exists
Policy exists but was approved more than 2 years ago without review
Policy does not address non-electronic (paper) personal data
Policy does not include breach notification procedures
suggestedSources: Data security policy document (current version with approval signature)
Policy version history and review log
Staff communication records showing policy distribution
evidenceItems.format: PDF / Word document with approval signature
evidenceItems.retentionPeriod: Current version plus all prior versions for 10 years

evidenceItems.name: Security risk assessment reports and remediation tracker
evidenceItems.description: Documented security risk assessments for systems processing personal data, with an active remediation tracker
whatGoodLooksLike: Risk assessment covers all systems processing personal data
Remediation tracker shows status of all findings with target resolution dates
High and critical findings are remediated within 30 days
Penetration test reports are included for internet-facing systems
Previous year's findings are shown as closed with evidence of remediation
commonGaps: No security risk assessment has been conducted in the past 2 years
Critical vulnerabilities remain open with no remediation timeline
Penetration testing has never been conducted on systems containing personal data
suggestedSources: Most recent security risk assessment report
Penetration test reports (most recent)
Vulnerability remediation tracker

evidenceItems.name: Security controls evidence (encryption, access controls, MFA)
evidenceItems.description: Technical evidence demonstrating that required security controls are implemented and operating effectively
whatGoodLooksLike: Encryption certificates for all databases containing personal data showing AES-256 or equivalent
TLS/SSL configuration showing minimum TLS 1.2 for all web-facing services
Access control matrix showing role-based access with least-privilege
MFA enforcement evidence for all privileged and remote access accounts
Quarterly access review records showing user accounts reviewed and removed where appropriate
commonGaps: Encryption at rest is not applied to the primary customer database or HR system
MFA is not enforced for remote access
Access reviews show significant numbers of former staff still with active accounts
Mobile devices used to access personal data are not encrypted
suggestedSources: Database encryption configuration screenshots
SSL Labs scan results for web services
IAM system access control matrix export
MFA enrollment report
Quarterly access review completion records

evidenceItems.name: Security awareness training records
evidenceItems.description: Records showing that all staff with access to personal data have completed mandatory security awareness training
whatGoodLooksLike: Training completion records show 100% participation among relevant staff in the past 12 months
Training curriculum covers: phishing, social engineering, clean desk, data handling, and incident reporting
Training records include completion dates and the training version completed
New staff receive security training before being granted access to systems containing personal data
commonGaps: No formal security awareness training programme exists
Training has not been updated to include breach notification obligations under s12B
Training is conducted for IT staff only, not customer-facing or administrative staff
Training is a one-time onboarding event with no annual refresh
suggestedSources: Training management system completion report
Training materials (course content, slides)
Staff completion certificates or acknowledgement records
evidenceItems.format: Training management system report / PDF certificates

auditTips: Ask for the Personal Data Protection Standards 2015 self-assessment checklist and walk through it with the CISO
Request the most recent penetration test report and focus on high or critical findings — are they remediated?
Request the access review records from the last quarter — how many accounts were modified or removed?
Ask: 'What happens if a staff member loses a laptop containing customer data?' to reveal breach response and device encryption policy

s10.title: Retention Principle
s10.auditorFocus: JPDP inspectors will look for a documented retention schedule and evidence that it is being actively applied. Particular scrutiny is placed on indefinite retention without a documented legal basis, and whether disposal is being carried out securely.

evidenceItems.name: Data Retention Schedule
evidenceItems.description: A documented schedule specifying the retention period for each category of personal data, the legal or business basis, and the approved disposal method
whatGoodLooksLike: Schedule covers all categories of personal data processed, cross-referenced with the ROPA
Each retention period is justified with a specific legal, regulatory, or business basis
Disposal method is specified for each category
Schedule has been reviewed by Legal counsel and approved by the DPO
commonGaps: No retention schedule exists — the organisation retains all personal data indefinitely
Schedule exists but has never been operationalised
Schedule does not cover data held in email archives, backups, or legacy systems
Retention periods are not justified with legal bases
suggestedSources: Data Retention Schedule document with legal counsel sign-off
ROPA cross-reference showing all data categories are covered
evidenceItems.retentionPeriod: Current version plus prior versions for 7 years

evidenceItems.name: Disposal certificates and deletion logs
evidenceItems.description: Records documenting the secure disposal or deletion of personal data that has reached the end of its retention period
whatGoodLooksLike: Disposal certificates from certified data destruction vendors for physical records
Electronic deletion logs from data management systems showing data purged with timestamps
Deletion runs are conducted on a regular schedule and documented
Backups and archives are subject to the same retention schedule as live data
commonGaps: No disposal has ever occurred — systems contain personal data from inception
Electronic deletion occurs but is not logged
Certified destruction certificates cannot be produced for physical records
Backups and email archives are excluded from the retention schedule
suggestedSources: Certified destruction certificates from data destruction vendors
System deletion logs showing automated purges
Backup management policy showing backup retention and deletion schedule

auditTips: Ask to see the oldest customer record in the primary CRM — is the retention period for that data justified?
Check whether backups are covered by the retention schedule — a common gap is personal data being retained indefinitely in backups
Request a disposal certificate from the last physical record destruction and verify the destruction method is appropriate

s11.title: Data Integrity Principle
s11.auditorFocus: JPDP inspectors will assess whether the organisation has reasonable processes to maintain data accuracy, including validation at point of collection, update mechanisms, and a functioning correction request process.

evidenceItems.name: Data quality controls documentation and test results
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that data quality controls are implemented in systems processing personal data
whatGoodLooksLike: System configuration showing input validation rules applied at collection
Duplicate detection configuration in CRM showing how duplicate contacts are identified and merged
Regular data quality reports showing key metrics such as percentage of records with valid contact details
Process for systematic update of records when data subjects inform the organisation of changes
commonGaps: No input validation at collection — data entry allows any format
Data quality has never been formally measured
Duplicate records abound in the CRM with no deduplication process
No systematic process for updating data when data subjects notify the organisation of changes
suggestedSources: System configuration screenshots showing validation rules
Data quality audit report (most recent)
CRM duplicate detection configuration

evidenceItems.name: Data correction request log and outcomes
evidenceItems.description: Records of all data correction requests received from data subjects, the organisation's response, and the outcome
whatGoodLooksLike: Correction request log records all requests with date, nature, response date, outcome, and third parties notified
Log shows response times within the 21-day period
Where corrections were made, evidence shows propagation to all relevant systems and third party notification
Where corrections were disputed, the log shows a correction statement was attached
commonGaps: No correction request log exists
Log shows a significant backlog of unanswered requests
Corrections are made in the primary system but not propagated to downstream systems
No DPO review or oversight of the correction log
suggestedSources: Data correction request log (last 12 months)
Evidence of correction propagation
Correspondence to third parties notifying of corrections

auditTips: Run a sample data quality check on a system containing personal data
Select 5 data subjects who have previously submitted correction requests and trace the correction through all systems
Ask to see a correction statement annotation on a record where the correction was disputed

s12.title: Access Principle
s12.auditorFocus: JPDP inspectors will assess whether the organisation has published and operationalised an accessible data subject access and correction request process, and test the organisation's ability to respond within the 21-day statutory period.

evidenceItems.name: Data Subject Access and Correction Request Procedure (published)
evidenceItems.description: The documented and published procedure for data subjects to submit access and correction requests
whatGoodLooksLike: Procedure is published in BM and English, accessible from the privacy notice
Procedure clearly states the 21-day response timeframe and any applicable fee
Contact details for submitting requests are current and actively monitored
Internal procedure document details the steps from receipt to response
commonGaps: No published procedure — privacy notice states access requests can be submitted but provides no contact details
Published contact email is unmonitered or inactive
Identity verification requirements are so onerous they effectively deter legitimate requests
suggestedSources: Published data subject access request procedure (website screenshot)
Privacy notice section on data subject rights
Internal access request handling procedure document

evidenceItems.name: Data subject access and correction request log
evidenceItems.description: A log recording all data subject access and correction requests received, with dates, outcomes, and response times
whatGoodLooksLike: Log captures all requests across all channels
Log shows date received, date acknowledged, date of full response, and outcome
Response times for all requests are within 21 days
Where access was refused, the specific exemption relied on is recorded
commonGaps: No request log exists
Log shows multiple requests not responded to within 21 days without any extension request
Log is incomplete — only email requests are captured
Requests that resulted in refusal do not show evidence of written refusal notification
suggestedSources: Data subject request log (last 3 years)
Sample access request responses (redacted)
Written refusal notifications where applicable

auditTips: Navigate to the organisation's website and attempt to find the data subject access request procedure
Request the access request log for the past 2 years and verify all response times are within 21 days
Select 3 sample access requests and request copies of the actual responses to verify completeness

s13.title: Registration of Data Controllers
s13.auditorFocus: JPDP inspectors will verify registration status at the outset of any inspection. A lapsed, expired, or absent registration when required is an immediate serious finding. Inspectors will also verify that the registered particulars match actual processing activities.

evidenceItems.name: Current PDPA Registration Certificate
evidenceItems.description: The organisation's current, valid registration certificate issued by the Commissioner
whatGoodLooksLike: Certificate is current (not expired) and issued to the correct legal entity
Certificate covers all relevant industry sectors
Renewal application is on file showing it was submitted before the expiry of the previous certificate
Digital copy is stored in the compliance document repository
commonGaps: Certificate has expired and no renewal has been submitted
Certificate covers only some of the industry sectors in which the organisation operates
Certificate is for a predecessor legal entity and has not been updated after a corporate restructuring
suggestedSources: Original PDPA Registration Certificate (physical and digital copy)
Renewal application and Commissioner's acknowledgement
JPDP online portal screenshot confirming current registration status

evidenceItems.name: Registration renewal compliance calendar and reminder evidence
evidenceItems.description: Evidence of a compliance calendar tracking the registration expiry date and generating advance renewal reminders
whatGoodLooksLike: Compliance calendar shows the registration expiry date with automated reminders at 90, 60, and 30 days
Named responsible officer for initiating the renewal with documented backup
Renewal is initiated at least 60 days before expiry
commonGaps: No compliance calendar — reliance on the responsible officer's memory
Renewal was submitted late because the responsible officer was on leave and no backup was designated
No record of previous renewal cycles
suggestedSources: Compliance calendar screenshots showing registration expiry and reminders
Historical renewal applications and Commissioner acknowledgements
Named responsible officer and backup documented in compliance manual
evidenceItems.format: Calendar screenshots / Email evidence / Process document

auditTips: Request the PDPA Registration Certificate at the start of the inspection
Verify the certificate is in the name of the correct legal entity
Check the expiry date — if it has expired, ascertain when the renewal application was submitted
Cross-reference the industry sector on the certificate against actual business activities

s14.title: Application for Registration
s14.auditorFocus: JPDP inspectors will verify that the registration application accurately reflects the organisation's processing activities. Inconsistency between the application and actual operations may indicate failure to notify changes.

evidenceItems.name: Registration application and ROPA consistency review
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that the information in the most recent registration application is consistent with actual processing activities as documented in the ROPA
whatGoodLooksLike: The processing activities, data categories, and purposes in the registration application match the ROPA
Where the ROPA was updated after the application was submitted, a change notification to the Commissioner is on file
Legal counsel or DPO sign-off on the registration application pre-submission is documented
commonGaps: Registration application describes significantly fewer processing activities than the ROPA
Change notifications have never been filed despite material changes since the last registration
No DPO or legal review of the application before submission
suggestedSources: Copy of the most recent registration application submitted to JPDP
ROPA (current version) for cross-reference
Change notification letters sent to the Commissioner
DPO or legal counsel sign-off on the application

auditTips: Compare the registration application against the ROPA — are there processing activities in the ROPA not mentioned in the application?
Ask when the organisation last reviewed the registration application for accuracy
Check whether any corporate events occurred after registration that should have triggered a change notification

s30.title: Right of Access to Personal Data
s30.auditorFocus: JPDP inspectors may submit a test data access request during or after an inspection. They will also review past request logs to verify compliance with the 21-day statutory deadline. The format, completeness, and language of responses are assessed.

evidenceItems.name: Sample access request responses
evidenceItems.description: Copies of responses provided to data subjects in response to data access requests
whatGoodLooksLike: Response includes all personal data held about the data subject across all relevant systems
Information is provided in an intelligible format with explanatory labels
Response is in a language the data subject can understand
Response is dated within 21 days of the original request
Where some data was withheld, the response explains the specific exemption relied on
commonGaps: Response only covers data from one system while information is spread across multiple systems
Response is a raw database export that is incomprehensible to a layperson
Response time exceeds 21 days with no Commissioner extension request on file
Refusals do not provide reasons or information about the right to complain
suggestedSources: Redacted sample access request responses (last 12 months)
Data subject request log showing response dates
Response template used by the organisation
Identity verification evidence before response

auditTips: Submit a test data access request during or after the inspection
Select 3 sample responses and assess completeness — does the response cover all systems in the data map?
Ask the organisation to demonstrate live how a data access request would be handled

s31.title: Compliance with Data Access Request
s31.auditorFocus: JPDP inspectors will review the request log to verify all requests were responded to within 21 days, and that any extensions were properly applied for.

evidenceItems.name: Data subject request management system or log with deadline tracking
evidenceItems.description: A systematic record of all data subject requests showing receipt dates, response dates, and evidence of deadline monitoring
whatGoodLooksLike: Every request has a clearly recorded date received and date responded, showing elapsed time
Automated deadline alerts are configured at day 14 and day 20
Where the 21-day deadline was not met, an extension request to the Commissioner predates the deadline
No requests have been simply abandoned or ignored
commonGaps: Request log shows multiple instances where the 21-day deadline was missed
Request log is maintained in a shared email inbox rather than a structured tracking system
Deadline tracking is manual with no automated alerts
Log shows 'completed' for requests that were only partially fulfilled without explanation
suggestedSources: Data subject request management system export (all requests, last 3 years)
Automated deadline alert configuration and alert history
Extension requests submitted to the Commissioner
Monthly DPO report on request handling performance

auditTips: Count the days between 'date received' and 'date responded' for every entry in the request log
For any 21-day deadline breach, verify whether an extension request was submitted before the deadline
Ask whether the organisation charges a fee and verify it is within the prescribed amount

s32.title: Right to Correct Personal Data
s32.auditorFocus: JPDP inspectors will review the correction request log and test whether corrections are propagated to all relevant systems. Third-party notification of corrections is frequently overlooked — inspectors will specifically look for evidence of this.

evidenceItems.name: Data correction request log with outcomes and third-party notifications
evidenceItems.description: A complete record of all data correction requests showing outcomes and evidence of third-party notification
whatGoodLooksLike: Log captures all correction requests with date received, nature, response, and outcome
Where corrections were made, evidence exists of the change and notification to relevant third parties
Where corrections were disputed, evidence exists of the correction statement annotation on the record
Third-party notification is documented with email or letter evidence
commonGaps: Corrections are made in the primary CRM but not pushed to integrated systems
Third parties who received the inaccurate data are never notified of the correction
Where the organisation disputes the correction, no correction statement is attached
No correction request log exists
suggestedSources: Correction request log (last 3 years)
Before/after screenshots showing data correction in primary and downstream systems
Email or letter correspondence notifying third parties of corrections
Correction statement annotations on disputed records

auditTips: Select 3 correction request log entries and trace each correction through all downstream systems
Ask the organisation to identify third parties who received the inaccurate data and show evidence of notification
Look for correction statement annotations on disputed records

s43.title: Processing by Data Processor
s43.auditorFocus: JPDP inspectors will verify that all data processors are covered by written DPAs and that those DPAs contain the mandatory terms. A gap in DPA coverage for even a single significant processor is treated as a serious compliance finding.

evidenceItems.name: Processor register and DPA coverage matrix
evidenceItems.description: A register of all data processors cross-referenced with executed DPAs, showing 100% coverage
whatGoodLooksLike: Register lists all processors with: name, processing service, data categories, DPA reference and date
Every processor in the register has a corresponding executed DPA dated before the commencement of processing
DPA coverage matrix clearly shows no processor has access to personal data without a valid DPA
commonGaps: Register exists but major processors are missing (e.g., cloud hosting, email service, payroll processor)
DPAs are in place for some processors but the organisation cannot identify when each was signed
Some processors are classified as independent data controllers when they are actually processors requiring a DPA
suggestedSources: Processor register (current version)
IT vendor list cross-referenced with the processor register
Executed DPAs for all processors
Procurement records showing all vendors given access to personal data

evidenceItems.name: Sample DPAs reviewed against s43 requirements
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that executed DPAs contain all mandatory terms required by s43
whatGoodLooksLike: DPAs explicitly restrict the processor to processing only on documented instructions
DPAs include confidentiality obligations binding the processor's personnel
DPAs specify minimum security standards and require breach notification within a defined timeframe
DPAs require deletion or return of all personal data at the end of the engagement
DPAs restrict sub-processing without prior written consent
commonGaps: DPAs are general-purpose service agreements without specific data protection clauses
DPAs do not include breach notification obligations
DPAs permit sub-processing without restriction
DPAs do not include post-contract deletion obligations
suggestedSources: Sample DPAs (at least 5, from different processor types)
DPA compliance checklist used by Legal
Standard DPA template approved by Legal counsel

evidenceItems.name: Processor due diligence review records
evidenceItems.description: Records of periodic due diligence reviews of data processors to verify implementation of security measures required by their DPA
whatGoodLooksLike: Annual due diligence questionnaires sent to each significant processor with responses on file
Security certifications (ISO 27001, SOC 2 Type II) from processors are on file and current
Where due diligence reveals non-compliance, a remediation plan is documented and monitored
commonGaps: No processor due diligence has ever been conducted
Due diligence is limited to collecting a questionnaire that is never reviewed
ISO 27001 or SOC 2 certificates on file are expired
suggestedSources: Annual processor due diligence questionnaire responses
Processor security certifications
Due diligence review reports

auditTips: Ask for the processor register and cross-reference it against the IT vendor list
Select 5 DPAs from different types of processors and check each against the s43 requirements checklist
Ask: 'Has any processor notified you of a data breach?' to reveal processor breach notification practice
